content: In the context of Municipal Solid Waste Management, ‘Haul’ in ‘Hauled Container System operated in conventional mode’ includes the _________.

options.content: time spent by the transport truck at the disposal site
time spent by the transport truck in traveling between a pickup point and the disposal site with a loaded container
time spent by the transport truck in picking up a loaded container at a pickup point
time spent by the transport truck in driving from the depot to the first pickup point

explanation:
In the context of Municipal Solid Waste Management, the term ‘Haul’ in ‘Hauled Container System operated in conventional mode’ generally refers to the time spent by the transport truck in traveling between a pickup point and the disposal site with a loaded container. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: time spent by the transport truck in traveling between a pickup point and the disposal site with a loaded container

content: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for the aerobic composting of sewage sludge?

options.content: Bulking agent is added during the composting process to reduce the porosity of the solid mixture
Leachate can be generated during composting
Actinomycetes are involved in the process
In-vessel composting systems cannot be operated in the plug-flow mode

explanation:
Let's analyze each of the provided options to determine which are true for the aerobic composting of sewage sludge:

Option A: Bulking agent is added during the composting process to reduce the porosity of the solid mixture

This statement is incorrect. Bulking agents are added to increase the porosity and improve the aeration of the mixture. They help to maintain an optimal oxygen level, which is crucial for aerobic microorganisms to decompose the organic matter effectively.

Option B: Leachate can be generated during composting

This statement is true. Leachate, which is the liquid that drains from the composting pile, can be generated during the composting process. It typically contains dissolved organic matter, nutrients, and possibly contaminants from the sewage sludge.

Option C: Actinomycetes are involved in the process

This statement is true. Actinomycetes are a group of filamentous bacteria that play a significant role in the decomposition of organic matter during composting. They are especially active during the later stages of composting and are known for breaking down more complex organic compounds.

Option D: In-vessel composting systems cannot be operated in the plug-flow mode

This statement is incorrect. In-vessel composting systems can indeed be operated in the plug-flow mode. In the plug-flow mode, materials move through the system in a continuous, orderly manner, ensuring a uniform composting process.

Thus, the TRUE statements for the aerobic composting of sewage sludge are:

Option B and Option C

content: A system of seven river segments is shown in the schematic diagram. The Ri’s, Qi’s, and Ci’s (i = 1 to 7) are the river segments, their corresponding flow rates, and concentrations of a conservative pollutant, respectively. Assume complete mixing at the intersections, no additional water loss or gain in the system, and steady state condition. Given: Q1 = 5 m3/s ; Q2 = 15 m3/s ; Q4 = 3 m3/s ; Q6 = 8 m3/s ; C1 = 8 kg/m3; C2 = 12 kg/m3; C6 = 10 kg/m3. What is the steady state concentration (in kg/m3, rounded off to two decimal place) of the pollutant in the river segment 7 ? ______
explanation:
Given :

  • $ Q_1 = 5 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $
  • $ Q_2 = 15 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $
  • $ Q_4 = 3 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $
  • $ Q_6 = 8 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $
  • $ C_1 = 8 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $
  • $ C_2 = 12 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $
  • $ C_6 = 10 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $

From the diagram :

  • $ Q_3 = Q_1 + Q_2 = 5 + 15 = 20 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $

Calculating concentration $ C_3 $ :

$ C_3 = \frac{Q_1 C_1 + Q_2 C_2}{Q_1 + Q_2} $

$ C_3 = \frac{(5 \times 8) + (15 \times 12)}{5 + 15} = 11 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $

Further calculations :

  • $ Q_5 = Q_3 - Q_4 = 20 - 3 = 17 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $
  • $ C_5 = C_3 = 11 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $
  • $ Q_7 = Q_5 + Q_6 = 17 + 8 = 25 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} $

Calculating concentration $ C_7 $ :

$ C_7 = \frac{Q_5 C_5 + Q_6 C_6}{Q_5 + Q_6} $

$ C_7 = \frac{(17 \times 11) + (8 \times 10)}{25} = 10.68 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $

Hence, the steady state concentration of the pollutant in river segment 7 is $ 10.68 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^3 $.

content:
The composition and energy content of a representative solid waste sample are given in the table. If the moisture content of the waste is 26%, the energy content of the solid waste on dry-weight basis is ________ MJ/kg (round off to one decimal place).

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Component } & \text { Percent by mass } & \text { Energy content as-discarded basis (MJ/kg) } \\ \hline \text { Food waste } & 20 & 4.5 \\ \hline \text { Paper } & 45 & 16.0 \\ \hline \text { Cardboard } & 5 & 14.0 \\ \hline \text { Plastics } & 10 & 32.0 \\ \hline \text { Others } & 20 & 8.0 \\ \hline \end{array} $$
explanation:
The composition and energy content of a representative solid waste sample are given in the table. If the moisture content of the waste is 26%, the energy content of the solid waste on dry-weight basis is MJ/kg (round off to one decimal place).

$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Component } & \text { Percent by mass } & \text { Energy content as-discarded basis (MJ/kg) } \\ \hline \text { Food waste } & 20 & 4.5 \\ \hline \text { Paper } & 45 & 16.0 \\ \hline \text { Cardboard } & 5 & 14.0 \\ \hline \text { Plastics } & 10 & 32.0 \\ \hline \text { Others } & 20 & 8.0 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

To determine the energy content of the solid waste on a dry-weight basis, follow these steps:

1. Calculate the weighted average energy content on an as-discarded basis:

$$ E_{\text{as}} = (20\% \times 4.5) + (45\% \times 16.0) + (5\% \times 14.0) + (10\% \times 32.0) + (20\% \times 8.0) $$

Simplifying, we get:

$$ E_{\text{as}} = \left(\frac{20}{100} \times 4.5\right) + \left(\frac{45}{100} \times 16.0\right) + \left(\frac{5}{100} \times 14.0\right) + \left(\frac{10}{100} \times 32.0\right) + \left(\frac{20}{100} \times 8.0\right) $$

$$ E_{\text{as}} = 0.9 + 7.2 + 0.7 + 3.2 + 1.6 = 13.6 \text{ MJ/kg} $$

2. Convert the energy content on an as-discarded basis to a dry-weight basis:

$$ E_{\text{dry}} = \frac{E_{\text{as}}}{1 - \text{moisture content}} $$

Given the moisture content is 26%, or 0.26:

$$ E_{\text{dry}} = \frac{13.6}{1 - 0.26} = \frac{13.6}{0.74} \approx 18.4 \text{ MJ/kg} $$

Hence, the energy content of the solid waste on a dry-weight basis is approximately 18.4 MJ/kg.
